cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The legal right to vote; in U.S. history, expanded from propertied white men to all citizens 18+ through constitutional amendments and legislation

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The percentage of eligible voters who actually cast ballots in a given election; the U.S. rate lags behind most other democracies and varies by election type

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: An organization of people sharing common goals that seeks to influence government by nominating and electing candidates to office; a linkage institution between citizens and government

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: An organized group that seeks to influence government policy without seeking elected office for its members; uses lobbying, litigation, and public advocacy

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: An organization that collects and distributes campaign contributions to candidates from corporations, unions, or other groups; subject to Federal Election Commission regulation

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: An independent organization that can raise and spend unlimited amounts on political advertising and advocacy but cannot coordinate directly with a candidate's campaign; created in response to Citizens United

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Laws and regulations governing how money is raised and spent in political campaigns; includes individual contribution limits, disclosure requirements, and restrictions on certain types of spending

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: An electoral system in which the candidate with the most votes (not necessarily a majority) wins; also called 'first-past-the-post'; used in most U.S. elections

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A primary election in which only registered party members may vote to select that party's candidates

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A primary election in which any registered voter may vote regardless of party registration, choosing which party's primary to participate in on election day

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The electoral advantage held by sitting officeholders, including name recognition, constituent services, access to media, fundraising advantages, and redistricting influence

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The boost in votes that a popular presidential candidate provides to lower-ballot candidates of the same party in the same election

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Voting for candidates of different parties in the same election (e.g., voting for a Republican president and a Democratic senator)

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Voting exclusively for candidates of one party in all races on the ballot

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A political party outside the two major parties; faces significant structural barriers including ballot access laws, the electoral college, and plurality voting rules

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The effect by which a third-party candidate draws enough votes from one major party candidate to cause the other major party candidate to win (e.g., Nader in 2000)

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Organizations (parties, interest groups, elections, media) that connect citizens to the government and policymaking process

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A voting model in which individuals cast votes based primarily on their evaluation of specific policy issues rather than party loyalty or candidate characteristics

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A voting model in which individuals make a calculated decision to vote for the candidate who best serves their self-interest, weighing costs and benefits

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A voting model in which individuals evaluate the incumbent based on the government's past performance, particularly economic conditions

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A voting model in which individuals vote based on which candidate's future promises best align with their preferences

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Attempting to influence government officials or legislators directly, typically on behalf of an interest group or organization, to affect policy decisions

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Interest group strategy of organizing ordinary citizens to contact their representatives or participate in demonstrations, creating visible political pressure from below

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The problem that prevents rational individuals from contributing to collective goods — since individuals can benefit from a public good whether or not they contribute, many choose to let others bear the cost

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The media's influence on what issues the public considers important, not by telling people what to think but by determining which issues receive coverage and prominence

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A type of campaign finance regulation limiting directly contributed funds to candidates; contrasts with 'soft money' not subject to the same federal limits

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Unregulated campaign contributions made to parties or groups for 'party-building activities' not directly tied to a specific candidate; banned by the Bipartisan Campaign Reform Act (2002)

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Coverage of elections focused on who is winning and losing rather than on policy substance; criticized for reducing campaigns to a competition narrative

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: 19th-century women's rights leader who co-organized the Seneca Falls Convention (1848) and spent decades fighting for women's suffrage; the 19th Amendment was nicknamed the 'Susan B. Anthony Amendment'

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Women's rights leader who co-authored the Declaration of Sentiments at the Seneca Falls Convention (1848), arguing for equal rights for women including the right to vote

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Independent presidential candidate in 1992 and 1996 who won 19% of the popular vote in 1992; demonstrated that third-party candidates can affect the outcome of elections and gain public visibility

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Green Party presidential candidate in 2000 who received about 97,000 votes in Florida; widely blamed by Democrats for drawing enough votes to cause Al Gore's loss in the state

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Ruled that political spending by corporations and unions is protected free speech under the First Amendment; effectively allowed unlimited independent expenditures in elections and led to the creation of Super PACs
cards.answer: Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Ruled that legislative redistricting is justiciable — federal courts can hear challenges to unequal district populations; opened the door to the 'one person, one vote' standard

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The U.S. has lower voter turnout than most other established democracies. Which of the following structural factors most directly contributes to this gap?

choices.text: Voter registration requirements in most states place the burden on citizens to register before elections rather than automatically enrolling them
choices.explanation: Most democracies automatically register citizens to vote; in the U.S., registration is a separate step with deadlines — this administrative barrier systematically depresses turnout especially among younger, more mobile, and lower-income citizens.

choices.text: The First Amendment prohibits mandatory voting laws that other countries use to boost turnout
choices.explanation: Several democracies have compulsory voting; the First Amendment does not prohibit such laws — no court has held that the Constitution bars mandatory voting requirements.

choices.text: American citizens are less educated about politics than citizens of other countries
choices.explanation: Political knowledge alone does not explain the structural turnout gap — countries with similar education levels have significantly higher turnout due to different registration systems and election structures.

choices.text: The Electoral College system discourages voting in non-swing states because those votes do not affect the electoral outcome
choices.explanation: While the Electoral College creates safe states, registration barriers are a more direct and empirically better-supported structural explanation for low overall turnout compared to other democracies.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Third-party candidates in the United States face significant structural barriers to winning elections. Which of the following best explains the primary structural barrier?

choices.text: The plurality voting system (winner-take-all) discourages voters from supporting third parties because votes for third parties are perceived as 'wasted' votes that help the least-preferred major party win
choices.explanation: Duverger's Law predicts that plurality voting systems produce two-party systems — rational voters avoid third parties because splitting the vote can cause their least-preferred candidate to win (the spoiler effect).

choices.text: The Constitution explicitly prohibits third parties from appearing on presidential ballots
choices.explanation: The Constitution has no such prohibition — third parties can and do appear on ballots, though ballot access laws (controlled by states) create practical barriers.

choices.text: Federal law bans campaign spending by third parties in all federal elections
choices.explanation: No such federal ban exists — the FEC regulates campaign finance but does not prohibit third-party spending. In fact, Citizens United expanded spending rights for all political organizations.

choices.text: Third parties lack access to television, making them unable to communicate their messages
choices.explanation: Third parties can purchase television advertising and participate in some debates — the fundamental barrier is the electoral system structure, not media access per se.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Interest groups use several strategies to influence policy. Which of the following is an example of inside lobbying (direct lobbying)?

choices.text: A lobbyist meets with members of Congress and their staff to provide policy research and draft legislative language
choices.explanation: Inside lobbying involves direct contact with government officials — providing research, testimony, and draft language are the classic forms of inside strategy, leveraging relationships with policymakers.

choices.text: A union organizes thousands of members to write letters and emails to their senators
choices.explanation: Mass constituent communication is grassroots mobilization (outside lobbying) — it creates pressure from below rather than directly engaging policymakers in one-on-one communication.

choices.text: An environmental group runs television advertisements attacking a senator's environmental record
choices.explanation: Electoral advertising to influence public opinion or elections is outside lobbying — it goes through the public, not directly to government officials.

choices.text: A corporation funds a public rally to demonstrate popular support for deregulation
choices.explanation: Public demonstrations are grassroots or outside strategies — direct lobbying involves private meetings with officials, not public demonstrations.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following best describes the electoral college's effect on presidential campaign strategy?

choices.text: Candidates concentrate resources and appearances in competitive 'swing states' while largely ignoring reliably partisan states
choices.explanation: Because winner-take-all rules in most states mean non-competitive states' results are predetermined, candidates have strong incentives to focus time, money, and advertising almost exclusively on the handful of states where the outcome is uncertain.

choices.text: Candidates must win a majority of the national popular vote to secure an electoral college majority
choices.explanation: Electoral college success and popular vote outcomes can diverge — as in 2000 and 2016 — because electoral votes are allocated by state, not national totals.

choices.text: Candidates spend equal time and resources in all 50 states to maximize their vote share nationwide
choices.explanation: The rational electoral college strategy is to concentrate on swing states — spending equal resources nationwide would be strategically irrational given the winner-take-all allocation system.

choices.text: Candidates focus exclusively on winning large population centers like New York and California
choices.explanation: New York and California are typically non-competitive (safely Democratic) — candidates ignore these states in favor of competitive states, regardless of their population size.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Citizens United v. FEC (2010) has been most criticized by opponents because it

choices.text: Allows wealthy corporations and unions to spend unlimited sums on elections, potentially drowning out the voices of ordinary citizens and creating the appearance of corruption
choices.explanation: Critics argue that unlimited corporate/union spending creates a system where money equals speech — wealthy entities can effectively dominate political messaging, creating unequal political influence regardless of the formal 'independent' nature of the spending.

choices.text: Eliminated all contribution limits to political parties, allowing unlimited direct contributions to candidates
choices.explanation: Citizens United addressed independent expenditures, not direct contributions to candidates — contribution limits to candidate campaigns were not eliminated by this ruling.

choices.text: Required all political donors to publicly disclose their identities, violating privacy rights
choices.explanation: Critics of Citizens United argue the opposite — that it enables 'dark money' through groups that can spend independently without full donor disclosure, not that it imposes excessive disclosure.

choices.text: Granted foreign corporations the right to participate in U.S. elections through Super PACs
choices.explanation: Citizens United applied to domestic corporations and unions — foreign spending in U.S. elections is still prohibited. Conflating foreign and domestic corporate spending misrepresents the ruling.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following most directly explains why political parties in the United States have become more internally unified and polarized along ideological lines since the 1960s?

choices.text: The sorting of ideologically conservative voters into the Republican Party and liberal voters into the Democratic Party, especially as white Southerners shifted to Republicans following civil rights legislation
choices.explanation: The Civil Rights Act of 1964 triggered a partisan realignment — conservative Southern Democrats became Republicans, making both parties more ideologically homogeneous and increasing the ideological distance between them.

choices.text: A constitutional amendment requiring all members of Congress to vote with their party on all legislation
choices.explanation: No such amendment exists — Congress members are not legally bound to follow party-line votes. Polarization is a product of electoral and social forces, not formal legal requirements.

choices.text: The creation of the Federal Election Commission in 1974, which imposed rules requiring party discipline on campaign finance
choices.explanation: The FEC regulates campaign finance — it has no mechanism for requiring party loyalty on legislative votes, and this is not the explanation for increased ideological polarization.

choices.text: The rapid growth in third-party membership that forced both major parties to adopt more extreme positions to compete
choices.explanation: Third parties in the U.S. have remained weak — the polarization of the two major parties is driven by internal sorting, not competition from third parties.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A voter decides to support the incumbent congressman after reviewing his strong record on reducing unemployment in the district. This is an example of which voting model?

choices.text: Retrospective voting — evaluating the incumbent based on past performance
choices.explanation: Retrospective voting involves judging incumbents based on their record — this voter is looking backward at what the representative accomplished rather than forward at future promises.

choices.text: Prospective voting — the voter is anticipating future policies the candidate will pursue
choices.explanation: Prospective voting focuses on future promises and platforms — this voter is evaluating past economic performance, which is the defining feature of retrospective voting.

choices.text: Straight-ticket voting — the voter supports all candidates of one party
choices.explanation: The scenario describes issue/performance-based voting, not party-line voting — the voter is responding to the specific incumbent's job record, not supporting an entire party slate.

choices.text: Issue voting — the voter is primarily motivated by the unemployment issue
choices.explanation: While unemployment is an issue, retrospective voting specifically involves evaluating past outcomes — issue voting typically involves selecting candidates based on policy positions, not past performance records.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following best describes the concept of media framing in political campaigns?

choices.text: The way media coverage shapes how audiences interpret political events by emphasizing certain aspects and minimizing others
choices.explanation: Framing refers to the media's power to influence interpretation by selecting which facts to highlight, which comparisons to make, and which context to provide — shaping what seems important or natural about a political situation.

choices.text: The legal requirement that media outlets present equal time to all political candidates
choices.explanation: Equal time rules are a specific FCC regulation — framing is a concept about how coverage shapes interpretation, not about airtime allocation.

choices.text: Campaigns purchasing advertising space to present their preferred image to voters
choices.explanation: Paid advertising is candidate messaging, not media framing — framing refers to how journalists and editors present stories in news coverage, not how campaigns buy ad time.

choices.text: The use of social media to bypass traditional news organizations and communicate directly with voters
choices.explanation: Direct candidate communication via social media is a disintermediation strategy — framing is specifically about how intermediary media organizations package and contextualize information.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following demographic groups has historically shown the highest voter turnout rates in U.S. elections?

choices.text: Older, college-educated, higher-income voters
choices.explanation: Age, education, and income are the three strongest predictors of voter turnout — older Americans vote at much higher rates than young people; those with more education and income have fewer barriers and stronger civic engagement.

choices.text: Younger, first-time voters who are most motivated by change
choices.explanation: Young voters consistently show the lowest turnout rates of any age group — enthusiasm among first-time voters does not overcome the structural and habitual factors that drive higher turnout in older cohorts.

choices.text: Working-class voters in rural areas who feel most strongly about economic issues
choices.explanation: Lower-income and working-class voters actually turn out at lower rates than high-income voters — economic anxiety alone does not reliably drive turnout compared to structural advantages of higher education and income.

choices.text: Voters with strong third-party affiliations who are motivated by ideological conviction
choices.explanation: Third-party voters constitute a small, ideologically motivated minority — high turnout as a demographic group is characterized by structural factors (age, education, income), not third-party affiliation.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The free rider problem most directly applies to which of the following political participation scenarios?

choices.text: A non-union employee who benefits from wage increases negotiated by a union they didn't join or pay dues to support
choices.explanation: The free rider problem: the employee benefits from the collective good (higher wages) without bearing the cost (union dues/participation) — a classic example of rational individual behavior that undermines collective action.

choices.text: A voter who votes for a third-party candidate even though they know it won't win
choices.explanation: Voting for a long-shot candidate is a form of expressive voting — it is not free riding because the voter is bearing the cost of participation (voting) and not gaining a private benefit at others' expense.

choices.text: A congressman who votes with his party to secure a committee chairmanship
choices.explanation: This is strategic reciprocal behavior within a political organization — the congressman is paying a cost (party loyalty votes) to receive a private benefit (chairmanship), which is transactional, not free riding.

choices.text: A media outlet that reports on politicians' private lives to attract viewers
choices.explanation: Media sensationalism is a market strategy — the outlet is providing content to attract audience, not benefiting from a public good while avoiding contribution costs.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following most directly explains why congressional incumbents typically win re-election at rates above 85% even when Congress as an institution has low approval ratings?

choices.text: Voters distinguish between Congress as an institution (which they dislike) and their own representative (who brings district benefits and is known to them)
choices.explanation: This 'collective versus individual' distinction explains the paradox — voters can disapprove of Congress overall while approving of their own member who delivers casework, pork, and visibility. Incumbents leverage name recognition and services.

choices.text: The Bipartisan Campaign Reform Act prohibits challengers from raising enough money to compete effectively
choices.explanation: BCRA (2002) limited some forms of soft money but did not prevent competitive challenger fundraising. Incumbency advantage predates and survives campaign finance changes.

choices.text: The Constitution requires that incumbents receive preference in redistricting to maintain experienced representation
choices.explanation: No such constitutional provision exists — redistricting can harm or help incumbents depending on who controls the process, and incumbency preference is not a constitutional requirement.

choices.text: Term limits prevent challengers from running against incumbents in general elections
choices.explanation: No term limits exist for U.S. senators or representatives — the 22nd Amendment limits only the president. Any eligible citizen can challenge an incumbent.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The media's role in setting the political agenda is best illustrated by which of the following?

choices.text: Extensive media coverage of immigration causes voters to rank immigration as a top policy priority even when immigration levels are unchanged
choices.explanation: Agenda-setting is the media's power to influence which issues people think are important through coverage intensity and prominence — not by telling people what to think about an issue but what issues to think about.

choices.text: A media outlet endorses a presidential candidate and tells viewers exactly how to vote
choices.explanation: Editorial endorsements are attempts to influence vote choice, not agenda-setting — agenda-setting is about which issues receive attention, not which candidates the outlet prefers.

choices.text: Voters ignore media coverage and research issues independently using government databases
choices.explanation: If voters ignore media coverage entirely, the agenda-setting effect would be absent — agenda-setting specifically requires that media coverage influences what issues voters consider important.

choices.text: A television network restricts its journalists from covering certain topics to protect advertiser relationships
choices.explanation: This describes self-censorship due to advertiser pressure — a media bias concern, but not the agenda-setting effect, which is about what issues receive coverage and how that shapes public priorities.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following best explains why political parties in the United States perform the function of 'simplifying choices for voters'?

choices.text: Party labels provide low-information voters with a reliable shortcut to identify candidates likely to share their general policy preferences without researching every candidate individually
choices.explanation: In a complex political environment with hundreds of races and thousands of candidates, party identification serves as a heuristic (mental shortcut) that allows voters to make reasonably reliable choices without becoming policy experts.

choices.text: Parties reduce choices by legally preventing more than two candidates from appearing on ballots
choices.explanation: Parties have no legal authority to prevent third-party or independent candidates from ballot access — the simplification comes from the cognitive shortcut parties provide, not from legally limiting choices.

choices.text: Parties make choices simpler by selecting the same candidates in every election through internal appointments
choices.explanation: Parties do not appoint candidates — they run primary elections (or sometimes caucuses) to select them. Candidate selection is competitive, not automatic appointment.

choices.text: Party platforms are legally binding documents that all candidates must follow once in office
choices.explanation: Party platforms are aspirational documents — they are not legally binding, and elected officials frequently deviate from platform positions without legal consequence.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following is the strongest predictor of voter turnout at the individual level?

choices.text: Age — older Americans vote at significantly higher rates than younger Americans
choices.explanation: Research consistently shows age as the single strongest demographic predictor of voter turnout — the gap between young (18-29) and senior (65+) voter turnout is often 20-30 percentage points.

choices.text: Religious affiliation — church attendance determines whether individuals vote
choices.explanation: While religious attendance correlates modestly with civic engagement, it is far weaker than age as a predictor of voter turnout across the general population.

choices.text: Geographic region — voters in the South always turn out at higher rates than voters in the Northeast
choices.explanation: Regional differences in turnout exist but are much weaker than the age effect, and the claim about Southern superiority is not empirically accurate — turnout patterns vary substantially within regions.

choices.text: Candidate quality — well-liked candidates cause dramatic increases in turnout
choices.explanation: Candidate popularity affects turnout at the margins — but individual demographic characteristics like age, education, and income are stronger and more consistent predictors of whether an individual votes.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: When analyzing an incumbent congressman's decision to vote against his party on a trade bill to protect manufacturing jobs in his district, which framework best explains this behavior?

choices.text: Electoral incentives — the congressman prioritizes district interests over party loyalty because local constituent needs drive re-election success
choices.explanation: This classic example of 'delegate model' representation vs. 'trustee' or 'party model' shows how electoral incentives create district-serving behavior — the congressman's job security depends on district outcomes, not party loyalty.

choices.text: Iron triangle — a bureaucratic agency forced the congressman to vote against his party
choices.explanation: Iron triangles describe policy alliances, not mechanisms that force votes against party. The congressman's behavior is driven by electoral calculations, not bureaucratic pressure.

choices.text: The seniority rule — senior members can vote against their party without consequences
choices.explanation: The seniority rule determines committee leadership positions — it doesn't grant freedom to vote against the party. The behavior is explained by electoral incentives, not seniority.

choices.text: Prospective voting — the congressman is anticipating future constituent reactions
choices.explanation: Prospective voting is a model of how citizens vote, not how legislators vote — the congressman's behavior is explained by electoral incentives and district representation priorities.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The development of social media has most significantly changed political campaigns by

choices.text: Allowing candidates to communicate directly with voters, mobilize supporters, and raise small-dollar donations without relying exclusively on traditional media intermediaries
choices.explanation: Social media fundamentally changed campaign communication — candidates can build direct relationships with supporters, organize volunteers, and raise money through digital platforms, reducing dependence on expensive television advertising and press coverage.

choices.text: Eliminating the influence of large campaign donors because social media is free for all candidates
choices.explanation: Social media has not eliminated large donor influence — major donors still fund television advertising and Super PACs. Social media is complementary, not a replacement for traditional big-money fundraising.

choices.text: Replacing the party primary system with direct social media votes on candidate selection
choices.explanation: Primary elections still use the official electoral infrastructure — no state has replaced formal primaries with social media voting. Social media is used in campaigns but not in the formal selection process.

choices.text: Requiring candidates to disclose all personal financial information in real time
choices.explanation: Financial disclosure requirements are set by law (the FEC), not by social media platforms — social media has no legal authority over disclosure requirements.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following best explains why interest groups sometimes achieve more policy influence than their membership size would suggest?

choices.text: Intense preference minorities — small groups with extreme passion for a narrow issue can mobilize more effectively than large, diffuse majorities with only mild preferences
choices.explanation: Political scientists call this the 'intensity problem' — a small group that would sacrifice everything for one policy can outmaneuver a large majority that slightly prefers the opposite but won't mobilize over it.

choices.text: Larger membership automatically translates to more congressional access because of one-person-one-vote rules
choices.explanation: One-person-one-vote applies to equal electoral representation in elections — it has no bearing on interest group access to legislators, which depends on resources, relationships, and intensity.

choices.text: Federal law gives interest groups formal veto power over legislation affecting their industry
choices.explanation: Interest groups have no formal veto power — they influence through lobbying, donations, and mobilization, but cannot legally veto legislation. Formal veto is held by the president.

choices.text: The free rider problem strengthens small groups by forcing all potential members to join
choices.explanation: The free rider problem actually weakens large potential groups — it makes it harder to organize large constituencies because members can benefit without joining. It does not strengthen small groups.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The Democratic Party's shift toward more liberal positions on social issues since the 1960s, and the Republican Party's shift toward more conservative positions, is best described as which of the following processes?

choices.text: Party sorting — ideologically consistent liberals clustering into the Democratic Party while conservatives cluster into the Republican Party
choices.explanation: Party sorting (or ideological sorting) refers to the process by which voters and politicians have reorganized so that each party is now more ideologically homogeneous — the ideological overlap that existed between liberal Republicans and conservative Democrats has largely disappeared.

choices.text: Dealignment — voters abandoning both parties to become independent
choices.explanation: Dealignment involves declining party identification — party sorting actually describes a strengthening of ideological consistency within parties, which is different from voters leaving parties.

choices.text: Realignment — the complete reversal of which party is dominant in national elections
choices.explanation: Realignment involves a lasting shift in which party dominates — the ideological sorting process can occur without a complete reversal in party dominance. The parties have sorted without one definitively becoming dominant.

choices.text: Partisanship — voters being more loyal to parties than in previous eras
choices.explanation: Increased partisanship is a consequence of sorting, not the process itself — sorting describes the ideological reshuffling within parties that preceded and caused increased partisan loyalty.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The Bipartisan Campaign Reform Act (BCRA) of 2002 attempted to address which specific campaign finance problem?

choices.text: The proliferation of 'soft money' — unregulated large contributions to political parties that were used for activities that indirectly benefited candidates
choices.explanation: BCRA (McCain-Feingold) banned soft money contributions to national political parties — these unregulated funds had become a major loophole allowing unlimited giving to parties that then spent on 'issue ads' that were functionally equivalent to campaign advertising.

choices.text: The establishment of Super PACs that could spend unlimited amounts independently of campaigns
choices.explanation: Super PACs emerged after Citizens United (2010) and McCutcheon v. FEC (2014) — they did not exist at the time of BCRA (2002) and were actually enabled by later court decisions that undermined BCRA's framework.

choices.text: The lack of public disclosure requirements for campaign contributions above $200
choices.explanation: Disclosure requirements for contributions above certain thresholds already existed before BCRA — the law's primary innovation was banning soft money contributions to national parties.

choices.text: The constitutional mandate preventing corporations from forming political action committees
choices.explanation: Corporations were allowed to form PACs before BCRA — the law targeted the specific soft money loophole, not PAC formation. Citizens United later addressed corporate independent expenditure limits.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following best explains the relationship between political parties and interest groups as linkage institutions?

choices.text: Both connect citizens to government, but parties seek to win elections and form government while interest groups seek to influence specific policies without taking office
choices.explanation: The key distinction: parties are electoral organizations that seek to control government by winning elections; interest groups are advocacy organizations that seek to influence government without seeking office themselves.

choices.text: Interest groups are more democratic than parties because they represent more diverse membership
choices.explanation: Interest groups actually face the collective action problem and often disproportionately represent wealthy, organized constituencies — they are not inherently more democratic than parties.

choices.text: Political parties provide legal advice to interest groups, while interest groups fund party operations
choices.explanation: This describes a purely transactional and legally questionable relationship — parties and interest groups have distinct roles as linkage institutions, not a legal services/funding arrangement.

choices.text: Both parties and interest groups are prohibited from contributing to presidential campaigns
choices.explanation: This is factually incorrect — parties can make coordinated expenditures to support their presidential nominees; interest groups can contribute through PACs. Neither is prohibited from presidential campaign involvement.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The Nineteenth Amendment's passage in 1920 is best understood as the culmination of which of the following?

choices.text: A decades-long social movement using lobbying, protests, and state-level campaigns to expand suffrage to women
choices.explanation: The women's suffrage movement began at Seneca Falls (1848) and culminated 72 years later — it used multiple strategies including state-level referenda, congressional lobbying, and public demonstrations to build sufficient political pressure for a constitutional amendment.

choices.text: A Supreme Court ruling that the Fourteenth Amendment already guaranteed women the right to vote
choices.explanation: The Supreme Court explicitly ruled in Minor v. Happersett (1875) that the 14th Amendment did NOT grant women the right to vote — a constitutional amendment was required, which is why the 19th Amendment was necessary.

choices.text: An executive order by President Woodrow Wilson granting women the franchise
choices.explanation: A constitutional amendment cannot be enacted by executive order — the 19th Amendment required passage by two-thirds of Congress and ratification by three-fourths of states.

choices.text: A single-year campaign launched by the National Organization for Women in 1919
choices.explanation: The women's suffrage movement spanned over seven decades — it was not launched in 1919, and the National Organization for Women was not founded until 1966.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following best describes how primaries have changed the presidential nomination process compared to the pre-primary era?

choices.text: Primaries have democratized the nomination process by shifting power from party bosses at conventions to rank-and-file voters, at the cost of sometimes nominating less electable candidates
choices.explanation: Before the 1970s reforms, party conventions controlled nominations — after the McGovern-Fraser reforms, primaries gave ordinary voters selection power, which increased democratic participation but sometimes produced nominees less capable of winning general elections.

choices.text: Primaries have strengthened party unity by ensuring all nominees share identical ideological positions
choices.explanation: Primaries can actually weaken party unity by producing ideologically extreme nominees who divide the party — competitive primaries can create intra-party wounds that damage general election prospects.

choices.text: Primaries eliminated the role of money in presidential campaigns by substituting grassroots competition
choices.explanation: Presidential primaries are extremely expensive — money remains critical to primary success as candidates compete across many states simultaneously.

choices.text: Primaries reduced the number of presidential candidates by legally restricting entry to established politicians
choices.explanation: Primaries actually increased candidate access by removing party gatekeepers — outsider candidates like Donald Trump (2016) and Bernie Sanders (2016) could compete in primaries in ways impossible under the convention system.
